attachment.content:
CAVEMAN MODE ACTIVE — level: full

Respond terse like smart caveman. All technical substance stay. Only fluff die.

## Persistence

ACTIVE EVERY RESPONSE. No revert after many turns. No filler drift. Still active if unsure. Off only: "stop caveman" / "normal mode".

Default: **full**. Switch: `/caveman lite|full|ultra`.

## Rules

Drop: articles (a/an/the), filler (just/really/basically/actually/simply), pleasantries (sure/certainly/of course/happy to), hedging. Fragments OK. Short synonyms (big not extensive, fix not "implement a solution for"). No tool-call narration, no decorative tables/emoji, no dumping long raw error logs unless asked — quote shortest decisive line. Standard well-known tech acronyms OK (DB/API/HTTP); never invent new abbreviations (cfg/impl/req/res/fn) — tokenizer split them same as full word: zero token saved, reader still decode. Full word cheaper AND clearer. No causal arrows (→) either — own token, save nothing. Technical terms exact. Code blocks unchanged. Errors quoted exact.

Preserve user's dominant language. User write Portuguese → reply Portuguese caveman. User write Spanish → reply Spanish caveman. Compress the style, not the language. No forced English openings or status phrases. ALWAYS keep technical terms, code, API names, CLI commands, commit-type keywords (feat/fix/...), and exact error strings verbatim — unless user explicitly ask for translation.

No self-reference. Never name or announce the style. No "caveman mode on", "me caveman think", no third-person caveman tags. Output caveman-only — never normal answer plus "Caveman:" recap. Exception: user explicitly ask what the mode is.

Pattern: `[thing] [action] [reason]. [next step].`

Not: "Sure! I'd be happy to help you with that. The issue you're experiencing is likely caused by..."
Yes: "Bug in auth middleware. Token expiry check use `<` not `<=`. Fix:"

## Intensity

| Level | What change |
|-------|------------|
| **full** | Drop articles, fragments OK, short synonyms. Classic caveman. No tool-call narration, no decorative tables/emoji, no long raw error-log dumps unless asked. Standard acronyms OK; no invented abbreviations |

Example — "Why React component re-render?"
- full: "New object ref each render. Inline object prop = new ref = re-render. Wrap in `useMemo`."

Example — "Explain database connection pooling."
- full: "Pool reuse open DB connections. No new connection per request. Skip handshake overhead."

## Auto-Clarity

Drop caveman when:
- Security warnings
- Irreversible action confirmations
- Multi-step sequences where fragment order or omitted conjunctions risk misread
- Compression itself creates technical ambiguity (e.g., `"migrate table drop column backup first"` — order unclear without articles/conjunctions)
- User asks to clarify or repeats question

Resume caveman after clear part done.

Example — destructive op:
> **Warning:** This will permanently delete all rows in the `users` table and cannot be undone.
> ```sql
> DROP TABLE users;
> ```
> Caveman resume. Verify backup exist first.

## Boundaries

Code/commits/PRs: write normal. "stop caveman" or "normal mode": revert. Level persist until changed or session end.

STATUSLINE SETUP NEEDED: The caveman plugin includes a statusline badge showing active mode (e.g. [CAVEMAN], [CAVEMAN:ULTRA]). It is not configured yet. To enable, add this to C:\Users\jande\.claude\settings.json: "statusLine": { "type": "command", "command": "powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File \"C:\\Users\\jande\\.claude\\plugins\\cache\\caveman\\caveman\\0d95a81d35a9\\src\\hooks\\caveman-statusline.ps1\"" } Proactively offer to set this up for the user on first interaction.

The question is if it should have a start and end color to enable the gradient, or how we would design it from the configuration. I want to understand if it is even possible and what kind of limitations there would be to execute this.

addedLines: - caveman:cavecrew-builder: Surgical 1-2 file edit. Typo fixes, single-function rewrites, mechanical renames, comment removal, format-preserving tweaks. Hard refuses 3+ file scope. Returns caveman diff receipt. Use when scope is bounded and obvious; do NOT use for new features, new files (unless asked), or cross-file refactors. (Tools: Read, Edit, Write, Grep, Glob)

- caveman:cavecrew-investigator: Read-only code locator. Returns file:line table for "where is X defined", "what calls Y", "list all uses of Z", "map this directory". Output is caveman-compressed so the main thread eats ~60% fewer tokens than vanilla Explore. Refuses to suggest fixes. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- caveman:cavecrew-reviewer: Diff/branch/file reviewer. One line per finding, severity-tagged, no praise, no scope creep. Output format `path:line: <emoji> <severity>: <problem>. <fix>.` Use for "review this PR", "review my diff", "audit this file". Skips formatting nits unless they change meaning. (Tools: Read, Grep, Bash)
- claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- compound-engineering:design:design-implementation-reviewer: Visually compares live UI implementation against Figma designs and provides detailed feedback on discrepancies. Use after writing or modifying HTML/CSS/React components to verify design fidelity. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:design:design-iterator: Iteratively refines UI design through N screenshot-analyze-improve cycles. Use PROACTIVELY when design changes aren't coming together after 1-2 attempts, or when user requests iterative refinement. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:design:figma-design-sync: Detects and fixes visual differences between a web implementation and its Figma design. Use iteratively when syncing implementation to match Figma specs. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:docs:ankane-readme-writer: Creates or updates README files following Ankane-style template for Ruby gems. Use when writing gem documentation with imperative voice, concise prose, and standard section ordering. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:best-practices-researcher: Researches and synthesizes external best practices, documentation, and examples for any technology or framework. Use when you need industry standards, community conventions, or implementation guidance. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:framework-docs-researcher: Gathers comprehensive documentation and best practices for frameworks, libraries, or dependencies. Use when you need official docs, version-specific constraints, or implementation patterns. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:git-history-analyzer: Performs archaeological analysis of git history to trace code evolution, identify contributors, and understand why code patterns exist. Use when you need historical context for code changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:issue-intelligence-analyst: Fetches and analyzes GitHub issues to surface recurring themes, pain patterns, and severity trends. Use when understanding a project's issue landscape, analyzing bug patterns for ideation, or summarizing what users are reporting. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:learnings-researcher: Searches docs/solutions/ for relevant past solutions by frontmatter metadata. Use before implementing features or fixing problems to surface institutional knowledge and prevent repeated mistakes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:repo-research-analyst: Conducts thorough research on repository structure, documentation, conventions, and implementation patterns. Use when onboarding to a new codebase or understanding project conventions. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:agent-native-reviewer: Reviews code to ensure agent-native parity — any action a user can take, an agent can also take. Use after adding UI features, agent tools, or system prompts. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:architecture-strategist: Analyzes code changes from an architectural perspective for pattern compliance and design integrity. Use when reviewing PRs, adding services, or evaluating structural refactors. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:code-simplicity-reviewer: Final review pass to ensure code is as simple and minimal as possible. Use after implementation is complete to identify YAGNI violations and simplification opportunities. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:data-integrity-guardian: Reviews database migrations, data models, and persistent data code for safety. Use when checking migration safety, data constraints, transaction boundaries, or privacy compliance. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:data-migration-expert: Validates data migrations, backfills, and production data transformations against reality. Use when PRs involve ID mappings, column renames, enum conversions, or schema changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:deployment-verification-agent: Produces Go/No-Go deployment checklists with SQL verification queries, rollback procedures, and monitoring plans. Use when PRs touch production data, migrations, or risky data changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:dhh-rails-reviewer: Brutally honest Rails code review from DHH's perspective. Use when reviewing Rails code for anti-patterns, JS framework contamination, or violations of Rails conventions. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:julik-frontend-races-reviewer: Reviews JavaScript and Stimulus code for race conditions, timing issues, and DOM lifecycle problems. Use after implementing or modifying frontend controllers or async UI code. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:kieran-python-reviewer: Reviews Python code with an extremely high quality bar for Pythonic patterns, type safety, and maintainability. Use after implementing features, modifying code, or creating new Python modules. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:kieran-rails-reviewer: Reviews Rails code with an extremely high quality bar for conventions, clarity, and maintainability. Use after implementing features, modifying code, or creating new Rails components. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:kieran-typescript-reviewer: Reviews TypeScript code with an extremely high quality bar for type safety, modern patterns, and maintainability. Use after implementing features, modifying code, or creating new TypeScript components. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:pattern-recognition-specialist: Analyzes code for design patterns, anti-patterns, naming conventions, and duplication. Use when checking codebase consistency or verifying new code follows established patterns. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:performance-oracle: Analyzes code for performance bottlenecks, algorithmic complexity, database queries, memory usage, and scalability. Use after implementing features or when performance concerns arise. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:schema-drift-detector: Detects unrelated schema.rb changes in PRs by cross-referencing against included migrations. Use when reviewing PRs with database schema changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:security-sentinel: Performs security audits for vulnerabilities, input validation, auth/authz, hardcoded secrets, and OWASP compliance. Use when reviewing code for security issues or before deployment. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:workflow:bug-reproduction-validator: Systematically reproduces and validates bug reports to confirm whether reported behavior is an actual bug. Use when you receive a bug report or issue that needs verification. (Tools: All tools)
- compound-engineering:workflow:lint: Use this agent when you need to run linting and code quality checks on Ruby and ERB files. Run before pushing to origin. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:workflow:pr-comment-resolver: Addresses PR review comments by implementing requested changes and reporting resolutions. Use when code review feedback needs to be resolved with code changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:workflow:spec-flow-analyzer: Analyzes specifications and feature descriptions for user flow completeness and gap identification. Use when a spec, plan, or feature description needs flow analysis, edge case discovery, or requirements validation. (Tools: All tools)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service — even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer — your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

attachment.content:
- context7-mcp: This skill should be used when the user asks about libraries, frameworks, API references, or needs code examples. Activates for setup questions, code generation involving libraries, or mentions of specific frameworks like React, Vue, Next.js, Prisma, Supabase, etc.
- ast-grep: Guide for writing ast-grep rules to perform structural code search and analysis. Use when users need to search codebases using Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) patterns, find specific code structures, or perform complex code queries that go beyond simple text search. This skill should be used when users ask to search for code patterns, find specific language constructs, or locate code with particular structural characteristics.
- code-changes: Orchestration workflow for any task that ends in code changes: issue analysis, pull request review, feature implementation, bug fixes, refactors, or fleshing out an idea. MUST be invoked at the start of such a task, before reading or writing any code. Defines how to analyze first, gate on user approval, plan, pick the right executor model, delegate and supervise subagents, verify, and deliver.
- conventional-commit: Workflow for generating conventional commit messages following the Conventional Commits specification. MUST be invoked every time a commit is created. Guides construction of standardized commit messages with correct type, scope, description, body, and footer.
- golang: Go coding standards and conventions for this project. Apply when writing, reviewing, or refactoring any Go source file.
- markdown: Markdown formatting rules for this project. Apply when writing or editing any .md or .mdx file, including documentation and website content.
- powershell: PowerShell cmdlet conventions for this project. Apply when writing or reviewing any .ps1 or module file.
- writing-clearly-and-concisely: Apply whenever writing content a human will read — emails, documents, reports, documentation, messages, UI copy, commit messages, explanations, or any other prose. Applies Strunk's timeless rules for clearer, stronger, more professional writing.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- caveman:caveman-commit: Generate terse caveman-style commit message
- caveman:caveman-init: Drop the always-on caveman activation rule into the current repo for every IDE agent
- caveman:caveman-review: One-line code review comments
- caveman:caveman-stats: Real session token usage + lifetime savings + USD. Tweetable line via --share.
- caveman:caveman: Switch caveman intensity level (lite/full/ultra/wenyan)
- caveman:cavecrew: Decision guide for delegating to caveman-style subagents. Tells the main thread WHEN to spawn `cavecrew-investigator` (locate code), `cavecrew-builder` (1-2 file edit), or `cavecrew-reviewer` (diff review) instead of doing the work inline or using vanilla `Explore`. Subagent output is caveman-compressed so the tool-result injected back into main context is ~60% smaller — main context lasts longer across long sessions. Trigger: "delegate to subagent", "use cavecrew", "spawn investigator/builder/reviewer", "save context", "compressed agent output".
- caveman:caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts output tokens 65% (measured) by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- caveman:caveman-commit: Ultra-compressed commit message generator. Cuts noise from commit messages while preserving intent and reasoning. Conventional Commits format. Subject ≤50 chars, body only when "why" isn't obvious. Use when user says "write a commit", "commit message", "generate commit", "/commit", or invokes /caveman-commit. Auto-triggers when staging changes.
- caveman:caveman-compress: Compress natural language memory files (CLAUDE.md, todos, preferences) into caveman format to save input tokens. Preserves all technical substance, code, URLs, and structure. Compressed version overwrites the original file. Human-readable backup saved as FILE.original.md. Trigger: /caveman-compress FILEPATH or "compress memory file"
- caveman:caveman-help: Quick-reference card for all caveman modes, skills, and commands. One-shot display, not a persistent mode. Trigger: /caveman-help, "caveman help", "what caveman commands", "how do I use caveman".
- caveman:caveman-review: Ultra-compressed code review comments. Cuts noise from PR feedback while preserving the actionable signal. Each comment is one line: location, problem, fix. Use when user says "review this PR", "code review", "review the diff", "/review", or invokes /caveman-review. Auto-triggers when reviewing pull requests.
- caveman:caveman-stats: Show real token usage and estimated savings for the current session. Reads directly from the Claude Code session log — no AI estimation. Triggers on /caveman-stats. Output is injected by the mode-tracker hook; the model itself does not compute the numbers.
- compound-engineering:agent-browser: Browser automation CLI for AI agents. Use when the user needs to interact with websites, including navigating pages, filling forms, clicking buttons, taking screenshots, extracting data, testing web apps, or automating any browser task. Triggers include requests to "open a website", "fill out a form", "click a button", "take a screenshot", "scrape data from a page", "test this web app", "login to a site", "automate browser actions", or any task requiring programmatic web interaction.
- compound-engineering:agent-native-architecture: Build applications where agents are first-class citizens. Use this skill when designing autonomous agents, creating MCP tools, implementing self-modifying systems, or building apps where features are outcomes achieved by agents operating in a loop.
- compound-engineering:andrew-kane-gem-writer: This skill should be used when writing Ruby gems following Andrew Kane's proven patterns and philosophy. It applies when creating new Ruby gems, refactoring existing gems, designing gem APIs, or when clean, minimal, production-ready Ruby library code is needed. Triggers on requests like "create a gem", "write a Ruby library", "design a gem API", or mentions of Andrew Kane's style.
- compound-engineering:ce-brainstorm: Explore requirements and approaches through collaborative dialogue before writing a right-sized requirements document and planning implementation. Use for feature ideas, problem framing, when the user says 'let's brainstorm', or when they want to think through options before deciding what to build. Also use when a user describes a vague or ambitious feature request, asks 'what should we build', 'help me think through X', presents a problem with multiple valid solutions, or seems unsure about scope or direction — even if they don't explicitly ask to brainstorm.
- compound-engineering:ce-compound: Document a recently solved problem to compound your team's knowledge
- compound-engineering:ce-ideate: Generate and critically evaluate grounded improvement ideas for the current project. Use when asking what to improve, requesting idea generation, exploring surprising improvements, or wanting the AI to proactively suggest strong project directions before brainstorming one in depth. Triggers on phrases like 'what should I improve', 'give me ideas', 'ideate on this project', 'surprise me with improvements', 'what would you change', or any request for AI-generated project improvement suggestions rather than refining the user's own idea.
- compound-engineering:ce-plan: Transform feature descriptions into well-structured project plans following conventions
- compound-engineering:ce-review: Perform exhaustive code reviews using multi-agent analysis, ultra-thinking, and worktrees
- compound-engineering:ce-work: Execute work plans efficiently while maintaining quality and finishing features
- compound-engineering:claude-permissions-optimizer: Optimize Claude Code permissions by finding safe Bash commands from session history and auto-applying them to settings.json. Can run from any coding agent but targets Claude Code specifically. Use when experiencing permission fatigue, too many permission prompts, wanting to optimize permissions, or needing to set up allowlists. Triggers on "optimize permissions", "reduce permission prompts", "allowlist commands", "too many permission prompts", "permission fatigue", "permission setup", or complaints about clicking approve too often.
- compound-engineering:deepen-plan: Enhance a plan with parallel research agents for each section to add depth, best practices, and implementation details
- compound-engineering:dhh-rails-style: This skill should be used when writing Ruby and Rails code in DHH's distinctive 37signals style. It applies when writing Ruby code, Rails applications, creating models, controllers, or any Ruby file. Triggers on Ruby/Rails code generation, refactoring requests, code review, or when the user mentions DHH, 37signals, Basecamp, HEY, or Campfire style. Embodies REST purity, fat models, thin controllers, Current attributes, Hotwire patterns, and the "clarity over cleverness" philosophy.
- compound-engineering:document-review: This skill should be used to refine requirements or plan documents before proceeding to the next workflow step. It applies when a requirements document or plan document exists and the user wants to improve it.
- compound-engineering:dspy-ruby: Build type-safe LLM applications with DSPy.rb — Ruby's programmatic prompt framework with signatures, modules, agents, and optimization. Use when implementing predictable AI features, creating LLM signatures and modules, configuring language model providers, building agent systems with tools, optimizing prompts, or testing LLM-powered functionality in Ruby applications.
- compound-engineering:every-style-editor: This skill should be used when reviewing or editing copy to ensure adherence to Every's style guide. It provides a systematic line-by-line review process for grammar, punctuation, mechanics, and style guide compliance.
- compound-engineering:feature-video: Record a video walkthrough of a feature and add it to the PR description. Use when a PR needs a visual demo for reviewers, when the user asks to demo a feature, create a PR video, record a walkthrough, show what changed visually, or add a video to a pull request.
- compound-engineering:gemini-imagegen: This skill should be used when generating and editing images using the Gemini API (Nano Banana Pro). It applies when creating images from text prompts, editing existing images, applying style transfers, generating logos with text, creating stickers, product mockups, or any image generation/manipulation task. Supports text-to-image, image editing, multi-turn refinement, and composition from multiple reference images.
- compound-engineering:git-worktree: This skill manages Git worktrees for isolated parallel development. It handles creating, listing, switching, and cleaning up worktrees with a simple interactive interface, following KISS principles.
- compound-engineering:proof: Create, edit, comment on, and share markdown documents via Proof's web API and local bridge. Use when asked to "proof", "share a doc", "create a proof doc", "comment on a document", "suggest edits", "review in proof", or when given a proofeditor.ai URL.
- compound-engineering:rclone: Upload, sync, and manage files across cloud storage providers using rclone. Use when uploading files (images, videos, documents) to S3, Cloudflare R2, Backblaze B2, Google Drive, Dropbox, or any S3-compatible storage. Triggers on "upload to S3", "sync to cloud", "rclone", "backup files", "upload video/image to bucket", or requests to transfer files to remote storage.
- compound-engineering:reproduce-bug: Systematically reproduce and investigate a bug from a GitHub issue. Use when the user provides a GitHub issue number or URL for a bug they want reproduced or investigated.
- compound-engineering:resolve-todo-parallel: Resolve all pending CLI todos using parallel processing, compound on lessons learned, then clean up completed todos.
- compound-engineering:test-browser: Run browser tests on pages affected by current PR or branch
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\code-changes

# Code Changes

The workflow for going from an issue, pull request, idea, or feature request to shipped code.
Follow the phases in order. Analysis always comes first; code comes last.

Each phase has a reference file with the full instructions. **Read the reference file when you
enter the phase** — not before, and never skip it because the phase "looks obvious".

## Roles

Assign work based on model capability. The coordinator is not the strongest model — it's the one
that stays resident, owns every phase by default, and knows when it's out of its depth.

| Role        | Capability tier                                               | Owns                                                                          |
| ----------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Coordinator | Capable mid-tier, resident for the whole task                | Analysis, plan, delegation, supervision, verification, delivery — by default |
| Escalation  | Strongest reasoning model available; invoked only on trigger | The specific judgment call the coordinator flagged, then control returns     |
| Implementer | Same tier as coordinator, or smaller for trivial edits        | Executing one pinned, self-contained task                                    |

Concrete model names per vendor (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) and how to run the split in Claude
Code, GitHub Copilot, Codex, or IDE agents: [references/model-tiers.md](references/model-tiers.md).

When the current agent already runs at implementer tier, there's no separate delegation step for
standard work: plan and execute directly, still following every phase. Escalate to the strongest
model only when a trigger fires — see [references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md) — never by
default and never for a routine judgment call the coordinator is equipped to make itself.

## The flow

1. **Analyze** ([references/analyze.md](references/analyze.md)) — root cause and scope,
   validated against the code, never against the report alone.
2. **Plan** ([references/plan.md](references/plan.md)) — pinned spec, task split, parallel vs
   sequential, workspace per task.
3. **Delegate** ([references/delegate.md](references/delegate.md)) — match each task to the
   right executor, coordinator-tier by default.
4. **Supervise** ([references/supervise.md](references/supervise.md)) — monitor, unblock, and
   critically review implementer output.
5. **Verify** ([references/verify.md](references/verify.md)) — quality gates plus functional
   proof, never delegated downward.
6. **Deliver** ([references/deliver.md](references/deliver.md)) — conventional commits and an
   outcome-first report.

Analyze, Supervise, and Verify each carry an escalation checkpoint — see
[references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md) — for handing one specific judgment call to the
strongest available model without giving up ownership of the phase.

**Stop gate:** Phase 1 ends with reporting the analysis and proposed approach to the user and
waiting for a go. Skip the gate only when the user already gave the go in the request itself
("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with Sonnet").

## Special cases

These entry points replace or extend Phase 1; the rest of the flow applies unchanged.

- Pull request review comments →
  [references/pr-review-comments.md](references/pr-review-comments.md)
- Issue triage → [references/issue-triage.md](references/issue-triage.md)

file.content:
# Phase 1 — Analyze

Start every task here, no matter how it arrived: issue link, PR number, verbal idea, bug report.
No code gets written or edited during this phase.

## Gather the full context

- Issues and PRs: `gh issue view <n> --comments` / `gh pr view <n> --comments`, plus linked
  issues, referenced discussions, and any code the report points at.
- Ideas and verbal requests: restate the goal and constraints in your own words. If the request
  is ambiguous, resolve the ambiguity now — not halfway through implementation.
- Check for prior art: existing helpers, similar segments/modules, and past commits that touched
  the same area (`git log -- <path>`).

## Reproduce before theorizing

Reproduce the problem when possible. A reproduction turns the analysis from a hypothesis into a
fact and gives Phase 5 its verification case for free. When reproduction is impossible (platform,
hardware, credentials), say so explicitly in the report and mark the fix as unverified-by-repro.

## Find the root cause in the code

- Read the actual implementation. Never reason from the issue text, a review comment, or a stack
  trace alone — reports and bot reviewers are frequently wrong.
- Distinguish the root cause from the symptom. Fixing where it crashes is not the same as fixing
  why it crashes.
- State what the change should be, which files it touches, and what it deliberately leaves alone.

## When to escalate

If root cause can't be pinned with confidence, or the fix looks architectural, security-sensitive,
or irreversible, hand the specific question to the strongest available model instead of guessing —
see [references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md). Resume ownership of the phase once the
question is answered.

## Output of this phase

A short analysis report to the user containing:

1. What is actually happening and why (root cause, with file references).
2. The proposed change and its scope.
3. What is intentionally out of scope.
4. Open questions, if any remain.

## Stop gate

Report the analysis and wait for a go before implementing. Skip the gate only when the user
already gave the go in the request itself ("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with
Sonnet"). A go given for analysis is not a go for implementation.

content.thinking:
I'm seeing how the terminal rendering works — the `Write` method processes text rune by rune with color overrides embedded in the text via anchors, and colors get resolved once per segment. Since the rune loop already handles per-rune output, I could inject gradient color escape codes before each rune to create smooth color transitions across the text.

But there are real constraints: TrueColor support varies (Apple Terminal doesn't have it, so gradients would degrade to banded 256-color output), and gradient endpoints need to resolve to actual RGB values rather than named colors. Each cell gets roughly 19 bytes of escape codes, which adds up quickly — a 40-character segment could balloon to 1-2KB with wrapping for bash/zsh prompts. I also need to account for display width using runewidth rather than rune count, since double-width characters would otherwise skew the gradient, and I need to know the total width before rendering starts, which means calculating it after template expansion.

The trickier part is how gradients interact with other features: color overrides like `<#fff,blue>` inside templates, hyperlinks, and styles all layer on top, making the gradient logic complex. Powerline symbols and diamond caps also pull their colors from adjacent segments, so with a gradient background, those connectors need to know the gradient's start and end colors — which means the gradient needs to expose those edge colors to the rendering engine.

For the config design, I could either extend the color string syntax to support something like `"gradient(#ff0000,#0000ff)"` or add dedicated fields like `background_gradient: {start, end, angle?}`. Since `color.Ansi` is a string used throughout templates and palettes, a string syntax would flow through existing code paths, but the problem is `ToAnsi` produces a single ANSI code and a gradient isn't one code — so gradient handling has to happen at the writer level instead. Let me look at how the engine actually writes segments and powerline connectors in the config and prompt files.

content.thinking:
It's definitely possible to implement gradient text in terminals since they support 24-bit color per cell, which means I can use escape sequences with interpolated RGB values for each character. The codebase already has precedent for this—the progress bar uses a scaled gradient approach, and go-colorful is already an indirect dependency. The main issue is that the current `color.Ansi` model produces a single ANSI code string, which doesn't align with what we'd need for per-cell gradient interpolation.

The writer architecture also needs rethinking. The terminal writer currently does a single pass through the rune loop, setting background and foreground colors once per segment. For gradients to work, I'd need to know the total visible cell width upfront to calculate the interpolated colors—but that information only becomes available after processing text anchors, hyperlinks, and accounting for double-width glyphs. So I'd need a two-pass approach: first scan to count cells, then emit the appropriate color escape sequence before each rune. The overhead would be roughly 15-20 bytes per cell per color channel, plus additional shell escape wrappers in interactive contexts.

Beyond the writer itself, adjacent elements like powerline symbols, leading/trailing diamonds, and the `parentBackground` keyword all need to know the gradient's edge colors. The current API returns a single background color, so I'd need to expose start and end colors from the gradient definition and update how segments report their edge colors for caps and parent keywords.

There's also the question of inline template overrides—when a gradient background is interrupted by a solid color override mid-segment, I need to handle the restoration cleanly when the override ends.

The simplest approach is to treat gradient as a per-cell default background at the writer level: for each cell, the writer either applies an active override color or stamps the interpolated gradient color. I also need to handle edge cases like transparent backgrounds with gradient foregrounds, and account for TrueColor support—Apple Terminal downgrades to 256-color palette, which causes banding in gradients.

For better color blending, I should use HCL or Lab interpolation instead of naive RGB, which go-colorful already supports through an indirect dependency. The trickier issue is width calculation in interactive mode where shell expansions happen after the gradient is computed, potentially mis-scaling the interpolation; I'll need to degrade or disable gradients on interactive segments. Multiline segments also complicate things since text can span multiple lines.

For the config design, I'm weighing two approaches: extending the color string syntax to support gradient notation like `"gradient(#FF0000,#0000FF)"` which reuses existing fields and plumbing but requires parsing inside the color string, versus adding explicit gradient fields which are cleaner for schema validation but require schema changes.

Option A seems better because it keeps a single source of truth—the color string remains the carrier, the writer detects the gradient prefix and switches to per-cell rendering, and existing helpers can parse the start and end colors for edge cases. This avoids duplicating precedence logic across background, templates, cycle, and foreground handling.

For multi-stop gradients like `gradient(#f00,#ffa500,#ff0)`, stops are evenly spaced with an optional direction parameter for later. The main tradeoffs are that per-cell escapes add some bloat to the prompt string (roughly 1KB per gradient segment), there's no gradient support in plain/no-color output, 256-color terminals will show banding, and interactive segments have unpredictable cell counts.

Cross-segment gradients that span multiple segments or whole blocks are much harder since they'd need the total block width before rendering, and the right prompt width isn't known until everything's rendered — that's a phase 2 problem requiring post-processing. I'm also thinking through how foreground gradients interact with hyperlinks, since hyperlink text writes raw runes without escapes.

The effort looks manageable: moderate changes to the writer and edge-color plumbing, plus schema and test updates. I should report this in full caveman mode and wait at the gate.

content.text:
Analysis done. Report:

## Verdict: possible. No terminal blocker — architecture blocker only, and solvable.

Terminal side trivial: 24-bit ANSI allows color change per cell. Gradient = interpolate RGB across visible cells, emit `\x1b[48;2;r;g;b m` (or `38;2` for foreground) before each rune. Prior art already in repo: progress bar uses bubbles `WithScaledGradient` ([model.go:12](src/cli/progress/model.go:12)), and `go-colorful` already indirect dependency — good interpolation lib (blend in Lab/HCL space, avoids muddy RGB midpoints).

## Where current architecture fights it

1. **One color per role assumption.** `color.Ansi` = single ANSI code, produced by `ToAnsi` ([colors.go:249](src/color/colors.go:249)). Writer state, color-override stack (`currentColor History`), keywords (`background`, `parentBackground`) all carry one code. Gradient = N codes — cannot flow through `ToAnsi`. Must be handled in writer, not color resolver.

2. **Writer is single-pass.** `terminal.Write` ([writer.go:356](src/terminal/writer.go:356)) sets colors once, then streams runes. Gradient needs total visible cell width *before* first rune to compute interpolation steps. Text contains anchors `<fg,bg>`, hyperlinks, style tags, double-width glyphs — so width needs pre-pass (same rune scan, count `runewidth`, skip anchors). Two-pass write. Mechanically fine, writer already decodes rune-by-rune.

3. **Neighbors need edge colors.** Powerline symbol foreground = previous segment background ([engine.go:484](src/prompt/engine.go:484)); diamonds, `parentBackground` keyword, transparent-foreground invert trick ([writer.go:564](src/terminal/writer.go:564)) — all consume one background color. With gradient they need **left-edge color** (leading cap) and **right-edge color** (trailing cap, next segment's `parentBackground`). Solvable: gradient carries explicit start/end stops, resolver returns correct edge per consumer.

4. **Inline template overrides** (`<#fff,blue>` mid-text). Override pushes solid color; on pop, must re-enter gradient at correct cell offset. Works if writer treats gradient as "default per-cell color" stamped each cell unless override active — no restore logic needed, just keep stamping.

## Config design — recommendation

Extend color string syntax, no new fields:

```json
"background": "linear-gradient(#FF0000, #0000FF)"
```

Reasons:

- Flows through everything untouched: `background_templates`, palette refs as stops (`linear-gradient(p:blue, p:pink)` — resolve each stop via `Colors.Resolve`), cycle, `foreground` gets it free.
- Separate `background_gradient` field would duplicate precedence logic everywhere (`cycle.Loop` overwrites `segment.Background` at [segments.go:197](src/prompt/segments.go:197), templates overwrite, caches key on the string) — every touchpoint would need dual handling.
- Multi-stop free: `linear-gradient(#f00, #ffa500, #ff0)`, evenly spaced stops.
- Keyword resolution: `parentBackground` resolving to gradient string — consumer parses edge stop where single color needed.

Writer detects `linear-gradient(` prefix, switches per-cell mode; everywhere else the string rides existing plumbing.

## Limitations (inherent, ship with them documented)

- **256-color terminals** (Apple Terminal — `TrueColor=false`): per-cell downgrade to nearest 256 = visible banding. Degrade, don't block.
- **Prompt string bloat**: ~15–25 bytes per cell incl. shell escape wrappers. 40-cell segment ≈ +1KB in PS1/PROMPT var. Harmless in practice, measurable.
- **Interactive segments**: shell expands text after render ([writer.go:531](src/terminal/writer.go:531) "UNSOLVABLE" comment) — cell count wrong, gradient scale off. Document: no gradient + `interactive`.
- **Cross-segment gradient** (one fade across whole block, agnoster-rainbow style): needs total block width before any segment renders — different beast, out of scope v1.
- **Plain mode / no color**: fall back to first stop.

## Scope if built

`src/terminal/writer.go` (two-pass + per-cell stamping), `src/color` (gradient parse + edge-color helpers + interpolation via `go-colorful` promoted to direct dep), `src/prompt/engine.go` (edge color at caps), JSON schema, docs, tests. Medium-size feature, no refactor of segment model needed.

Want me plan implementation, or analysis enough for now?

# Phase 2 — Plan

Turn the approved analysis into an executable plan. The quality bar: an implementer-tier model
must be able to execute each task without asking questions.

## Pin the spec

Write the spec down before delegating anything. It contains:

- The decided approach — including decisions already made, so the implementer does not relitigate
  them.
- Files to touch, and the entry points to start from.
- Constraints: style rules, patterns to follow (point at existing code), performance or
  compatibility requirements.
- Verification commands the implementer must run locally (build, tests, lint).
- Explicit non-goals: what the task must NOT change. This is what keeps subagents from wandering.

## Split into tasks

- One task = one self-contained unit an implementer can finish and verify on its own.
- Mark which tasks are independent and which consume another task's output. Parallelize the
  independent ones; sequence the rest. When in doubt, sequence — a merge conflict between two
  parallel subagents costs more than the parallelism saves.
- Documentation updates belong to the task that changes the behavior, not to a separate task.

## Decide the workspace per task

- Main working tree: when the task depends on uncommitted local changes, or when you will review
  and commit the result in the current session.
- Isolated worktree: everything else, especially parallel tasks — they must never share a
  working tree.

## Output of this phase

A task list where each entry names its executor tier (see Phase 3), its workspace, its
dependencies, and carries its pinned spec.

file.content:
# Phase 3 — Delegate

Match the executor to the task, not the other way around. The point is cost and speed without
losing quality — the coordinator stays accountable for the result.

## Executor matrix

Tier definitions and per-vendor model examples: [model-tiers.md](model-tiers.md).

| Task profile                                             | Executor                                                       |
| ---------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Trivial: mechanical edit, config tweak, typo, doc update  | Do it directly, or batch on a trivial-tier model              |
| Standard, well-specified implementation                   | Coordinator itself, or a same-tier subagent for parallelism   |
| Hits an escalation trigger (see [escalate.md](escalate.md)) | Escalation-tier subagent, for that one specific question    |

- The coordinator typically runs at implementer tier itself, so standard tasks are usually
  executed directly rather than delegated. Delegate anyway when there's real parallelism —
  independent tasks, each in its own worktree — or to shed trivial mechanical work onto a
  cheaper model.
- Escalation is never the default path for "ambiguous" or "architectural" — it fires only on the
  concrete triggers in [escalate.md](escalate.md), and only for the specific question, not the
  whole task.

## What a delegation carries

Hand the subagent its full pinned spec from Phase 2, plus:

- The verification commands it must run and pass before reporting done.
- The instruction to report what it changed and what it verified — not just "done".
- The instruction to stop and report when it hits something the spec does not cover, instead of
  improvising scope.

## What is never delegated downward

Analysis, final verification, and delivery never go to an implementer — they stay with the
coordinator or go up to Escalation tier for a specific question:

- Analysis and root-cause work (Phase 1).
- Final verification (Phase 5) — an implementer's green run is a claim, not a result.
- Commits, history rewrites, pushes, and user-facing reporting (Phase 6) — these stay with the
  coordinator regardless of tier; they're about accountability, not capability, so they never go
  to Escalation tier either.

content.thinking:
I need to nail down a few implementation details before writing the spec. The gradient parsing logic should live in a new file, and I'll need to handle the linear-gradient syntax by parsing stops and resolving them through the color palette to RGB values. The tricky part is that named ANSI colors can't be interpolated, so stops need to be convertible to hex format.

For the writer changes, I can't run gradient detection through the existing asAnsiColors function since that would destroy the gradient string. Instead, I need to check for gradient prefixes in the background and foreground values before that step, then pre-compute the visible cell width and build per-cell ANSI codes. The implementation will need a pre-pass that scans through the runes without emitting anything, stripping leading anchors and skipping over anchor markers.

For the stamping logic, I'll create a helper function to measure visible width by replicating the rune-scanning loop (accounting for anchors and hyperlink text while treating styles as zero-width), then in the write function itself, when a gradient is active and there's no color override, I'll stamp the per-cell color before each rune and advance the cell index by its width. Wide runes get the same color applied to both cells with a single escape sequence.

Handling gradient backgrounds and foregrounds separately... Resolving gradient keywords centrally in asAnsiColors by picking the appropriate edge stop based on context — leading diamonds use the first stop, while trailing diamonds and parent backgrounds use the last stop. The engine passes context information to guide which edge to select when a gradient keyword is encountered. The key insight is that `ParentBackground` and `ParentForeground` keywords should resolve to the last stop of a gradient, while `Background` and `Foreground` resolve to the first stop — except for the trailing diamond's final case where the engine explicitly substitutes the last stop. I can handle this by having `adjustTrailingDiamondColorOverrides` rewrite the background keyword to parentBackground for the trailing diamond, and then the resolution logic applies the right stop based on which keyword is being used.

For interpolation, I'm using HCL blending via colorful's `BlendHcl` function, and for cells I calculate the interpolation parameter as `cellIndex / (totalCells - 1)` so a single cell maps to the first stop. When TrueColor is disabled, I downgrade the computed RGB values to 256-color using gookit's color conversion. I'll also need to document this edge-case behavior in the spec and add test coverage.

Now I'm adding schema support for linear-gradient patterns in the color definitions and creating a docs page for the configuration. For testing, I'm writing comprehensive tests for parsing, edge cases, interpolation, and 256-color fallback, plus terminal writer tests with golden escape strings for various gradient scenarios and engine tests for powerline caps. I'll also add benchmarks to ensure the non-gradient path doesn't regress.

I need to run verification with the build and test commands across the color and terminal packages, plus linting and formatting checks. Breaking this into tasks: T1 handles gradient parsing and interpolation in the color package, T2 adds the two-pass terminal writer logic, T3 wires up the prompt engine for edge colors and parent cap handling, and T4 covers schema and docs. I can run T1 first, then parallelize T2 and T3 since they touch different packages, though T3's keyword resolution for parent colors might need coordination with the color package.

Sequencing T1 → T2 → T3 → T4 makes sense because engine tests that exercise the full gradient rendering pipeline depend on the writer being complete, and docs should be bundled with the final task that brings everything together.

All tasks stay on Sonnet 5 for consistency. Now I need to write the pinned spec and launch the agents, making sure they run verification and report changes before proceeding. Now I'm pinning down the color package API so all the agents stay aligned. I need methods to detect gradients, extract their stops, get the first and last colors, and a function to interpolate gradient stops across a given number of cells to produce per-cell hex colors ready for rendering.

The resolver needs to handle palette references like `p:name` by delegating to the Colors interface, and invalid stops get skipped — if fewer than two valid stops remain, the gradient falls back to a solid color. I'm thinking the color package should expose a simple `ParseGradient` function that validates the syntax and extracts stops, letting the writer handle the actual color resolution.

For keyword resolution, I need to be careful: when a keyword like `Parent*` resolves to a gradient, it should return the last color of that gradient, while `Background` or `Foreground` keywords return the first. But here's the tricky part — if a segment's own text background is a gradient, that gradient must stay intact through the write process, so keyword resolution can't collapse gradients for background or foreground contexts.

When handling diamonds and gradients together, the leading diamond with a foreground gradient should naturally use the first stop of that gradient, which works automatically. For trailing diamonds with a foreground gradient though, I need to explicitly pass the last stop to the engine's separator logic and color resolution functions to get the right endpoint.

The key insight is that gradients should only be active in the default path of writeSegmentColors—everywhere else collapses to either the first or last stop via GradientFirst(). This keeps things safe and predictable. For transparent foreground with a gradient background, I need to collapse to the first stop when converting the background to foreground, since ToForeground() on a gradient string would break.

When handling per-cell stamping with overrides, the writer tracks whether a gradient background is currently active and whether an override has suppressed it. If an inline foreground override is set, the foreground changes but the gradient background continues stamping per cell. If an inline background override is set, that override takes precedence and gradient stamping pauses. I can implement this by having writeAnchorOverride set a suppressGradientBg flag when an explicit background is provided, then endColorOverride clears it when the stack returns to base. The behavior pins down via tests: a gradient background with text should interpolate the color codes per cell from first to last.

For mid-text overrides like `<#ff0000,#00ff00>b</>`, the override cells get a solid green background and red foreground, then after the close tag the gradient resumes at the correct cell index since cell counting continues throughout. Foreground gradients don't affect other styles like bold. Wide runes emit one escape code and increment the cell index by two, with both cells receiving the same color.

Across newlines in segment text, the cell index continues without reset—the newline itself has zero width. Performance-wise, the non-gradient path must not regress, so I'll run the existing benchmarks and compare against main using benchstat. The hot path check for gradients is just a cheap string prefix check on segment colors, evaluated once per Write call.

I need to find the color definitions pattern in themes/schema.json and check the docs at website/docs/configuration/colors.mdx, then write the spec files to the scratchpad and launch T1 Sonnet.

input.content:
# Pinned spec — gradient-colored segments

## Feature

Segment `foreground`/`background` (and templates/palette/cycle values) may be a gradient:

```json
"background": "linear-gradient(#FF0000, #0000FF)"
```

- 2..N stops, evenly spaced, comma-separated inside `linear-gradient(...)`.
- Stops may be hex (`#RRGGBB`), palette refs (`p:name`), or anything `Colors.Resolve` turns into hex.
- Rendering: interpolate per visible terminal cell across the segment text, emit a truecolor
  ANSI escape per cell (`48;2;r;g;b` background, `38;2;r;g;b` foreground).
- Interpolation in HCL space via `github.com/lucasb-eyer/go-colorful` (already an indirect
  dependency of src/go.mod — promote to direct).
- `color.TrueColor == false` (Apple Terminal): downgrade each interpolated color to 256-color
  via gookit `RGB(...).C256()`. Banding accepted.
- Plain mode: no escapes at all (existing behavior, nothing to do).

## Decided architecture (do not relitigate)

1. Gradient stays a plain string riding the existing `color.Ansi` plumbing (config, templates,
   palette, cycle, caches). NO new config fields.
2. `Colors.ToAnsi`/`asAnsiColors` must never mangle a gradient string — pass through untouched;
   the terminal writer detects and renders it.
3. Gradient is only rendered per-cell in the DEFAULT path of `writeSegmentColors`
   (src/terminal/writer.go:546). Every other consumer collapses the gradient to a single edge
   color:
   - `parentBackground`/`parentForeground` keyword resolution → LAST stop.
   - `background`/`foreground` keyword resolution → keep gradient (the same segment's writer
     needs it); collapse happens downstream where a single color is required.
   - Powerline separator color (`getPowerlineColor`, src/prompt/engine.go:484) → LAST stop.
   - Final trailing diamond (engine.go:409 area) → LAST stop.
   - Transparent-foreground invert path and `writeTransparentStart` → FIRST stop.
   - Any remaining single-color sink → FIRST stop (safe default).
4. Invalid gradient (unparseable, <2 resolvable stops) → collapse to first stop if that stop is
   a valid color, else empty color. Never crash, never emit garbage.
5. Writer does a pre-pass over the segment text to count visible cells (runewidth per rune,
   anchors `<...>` zero, style tags zero, hyperlink URL part zero), then streams as today,
   stamping the interpolated color before each visible rune. Wide rune = one escape, advances
   cell index by its width. Newlines: zero width, cell index continues (no per-line restart).
6. Inline overrides: an explicit override color (e.g. `<red,blue>`) wins while active; gradient
   stamping for that channel suspends but the CELL INDEX keeps advancing; after `</>` the
   gradient resumes at the correct offset. An override that only sets the other channel leaves
   this channel's gradient stamping active.
7. Length/width accounting (`length`, `Len()`) unchanged — escapes are never counted.

## Cross-package API (pinned)

In `src/color` (new file `gradient.go`):

```go
func (c Ansi) IsGradient() bool                  // strings.HasPrefix "linear-gradient("
func (c Ansi) GradientStops() []Ansi             // syntax parse only, trimmed, nil if not/invalid
func (c Ansi) GradientFirst() Ansi               // first stop; returns c unchanged if not gradient
func (c Ansi) GradientLast() Ansi                // last stop; returns c unchanged if not gradient

// GradientCells resolves each stop through resolver (palette etc.), parses hex, interpolates
// across `cells` cells in HCL space, and returns one ready-to-print ANSI code per cell,
// honoring TrueColor (truecolor escape or C256 fallback). Returns nil when the gradient is
// invalid (caller falls back per rule 4). cells == 1 → first stop only.
func GradientCells(c Ansi, cells int, resolver String, isBackground bool) []Ansi
```

Internal helpers free to vary; this surface is what writer/engine consume.

## Constraints

- Follow the `golang` skill (project Go standards). Module root is `src/` — run all Go commands
  from there.
- Comment style: match existing files — comments state constraints, not narration.
- Hot path: the non-gradient path must not regress. One `IsGradient` prefix check per
  `terminal.Write` call is the budget. Benchmarks exist in `src/terminal/bench_test.go`.
- No new allocations in the non-gradient path.
- Do not touch: shell init scripts, segment implementations, `src/cli/progress` (its gradient is
  unrelated bubbles code).

## Verification (run from `src/`, must pass before reporting done)

```
go build ./...
go test ./color/... ./terminal/... ./prompt/...
gofmt -l . | grep -v vendor   (must be empty; or use gofmt -l on touched dirs)
```

For writer changes additionally: `go test -bench . -benchtime 10x ./terminal/` runs clean.

## Non-goals (v1)

- Cross-segment/whole-block gradient continuity.
- Angle/direction parameters (`to right`, degrees) — syntax reserved but only the plain
  stop-list form is implemented.
- Gradient on `interactive` segments (shell-expanded text skews cell count) — document as
  unsupported, no code guard needed beyond what falls out naturally.
- Gradient in transparent-foreground invert trick — collapses to first stop (rule 3).

file.content:
# Phase 4 — Supervise

Delegation is not fire-and-forget. The coordinator tracks delivery and owns the outcome.

## Monitor and unblock

- Track each subagent's progress against its spec.
- When a subagent stalls or loops on a problem: stop it, diagnose the problem yourself, hand it
  the answer, and let it proceed. Do not let it burn turns rediscovering what you already know.
- When a subagent reports a spec gap, decide — update the spec or cut the scope — and send it
  back with the decision. Never let it decide scope on its own.

## Review the output critically

Review every subagent diff as if it were an external PR:

- Check the diff against the spec: everything asked for, nothing beyond it.
- Override solutions that are wrong or overbuilt. Prefer the change that removes code over the
  one that adds it. It is normal to keep a subagent's diagnosis but replace its fix with a
  simpler one — document the override and its reason for the final report.
- Watch for spec-compliant-but-ugly: a change can satisfy the letter of the spec and still not
  belong in the codebase. Consistency with surrounding code wins.

## Escalate on low confidence

If a diff leaves you unsure whether the fix is correct or merely plausible, or it touches
security, data-migration, or otherwise irreversible territory, get a second read from the
strongest available model before signing off — see
[references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md). Don't rubber-stamp a diff you can't fully
verify yourself.

## Trust nothing unverified

"Tests pass" from a subagent is a claim, not a result. Phase 5 re-verifies everything
independently, on the merged state — not per-task.

Looking at the gradient implementation, the `GradientCells` function correctly returns the first stop when there's only one cell, matching the spec. The multi-stop position math handles the segment range properly with appropriate clamping, and both `BlendHcl` and the clamped values are working as expected. I'm noticing an issue with the resolver though — when handling hex color stops, `Defaults.Resolve` returns them unchanged, while `PaletteColors.Resolve` properly resolves palette references, but `colorful.Hex` expects the strict "#RRGGBB" format.

Actually, checking the colorful library more carefully — it uses `fmt.Sscanf` with `%x` which is case-insensitive for hex digits, and it also supports the short three-digit form like "#abc", so that's fine. The `PaletteColors.Resolve` method should handle non-palette references correctly by returning them unchanged. In keywords.go, `GradientLast()` is applied consistently at both exit points from `resolveParentColor`, including the default branch around line 43, which is correct since only Parent-prefixed keywords flow through that function. The `resolveKeyword` function for Background and Foreground returns the current gradient intact, which matches the spec. The `Cached` decorator properly caches the gradient-to-gradient mapping, and colorful handles three-digit hex codes without issue. I should run the build and color tests to verify everything works as expected.

input.description: T2: terminal writer gradient rendering
input.prompt:
You are implementing task T2 of a gradient-segment feature in the oh-my-posh repository at D:\oh-my-posh. Task T1 (color package) is DONE and merged in the working tree — the API in src/color/gradient.go exists and is tested.

First read the pinned spec: C:\Users\jande\AppData\Local\Temp\claude\D--oh-my-posh\dcce4143-4609-43c5-a0e3-ea883f6c959b\scratchpad\gradient-spec.md. Follow it exactly; do not relitigate decisions. Also read src/color/gradient.go for the exact T1 API, and invoke the project `golang` skill before writing code.

## T2 scope (ONLY src/terminal/writer.go + its tests)

Make `terminal.Write` render gradient foreground/background per visible cell.

### Behavior

1. In `Write`, after `asAnsiColors` (which now passes gradient strings through untouched), detect `backgroundColor.IsGradient()` / `foregroundColor.IsGradient()`.
2. When a gradient is present for a channel:
   - Pre-pass: count the segment text's visible cells BEFORE streaming. The count must match exactly what the existing rune loop would add to `length`: `runewidth.RuneWidth` per visible rune; anchors (`<...>` color/style/reset tokens via `scanAnchor`/`knownStyles`), hyperlink URL/markup parts, and the leading anchor contribute zero; the hyperlink no-text fallback contributes 4 ("link"); runes inside the hyperlink URL part (`isHyperlink == true`) contribute zero. Factor the counting so it cannot drift from the write path (shared helper or a counting pass over the same scan logic — your choice, but no duplicated divergent logic).
   - Call `color.GradientCells(gradientValue, cells, Colors, isBackground)` once per channel. nil result → collapse: replace the channel color with `GradientFirst()` resolved through `asAnsiColors` semantics and render as today (no per-cell mode).
   - In the streaming loop, before each visible rune, when the channel's gradient is active and not suppressed, emit the cell's color via `writeColorise(cellColors[cellIndex])`. Wide rune: one escape, then advance cellIndex by the rune's width. Newline: zero width, index continues. The stamped escape replaces the single up-front color for that channel in `writeSegmentColors` (do not emit a whole-segment color for a gradient channel there).
3. Gradient only renders in the DEFAULT path of `writeSegmentColors`. The transparent-foreground paths (both `fg.IsTransparent()` cases) must collapse any gradient via `GradientFirst()` before use — including the `bg.ToForeground()` call, which must never see a gradient string.
4. Inline overrides (`writeAnchorOverride` / `endColorOverride`):
   - An explicit override for a channel suppresses that channel's gradient stamping while active; cellIndex keeps advancing. Overriding only one channel leaves the other channel's gradient stamping active.
   - On restore (`endColorOverride`) back to a gradient base for a channel: do NOT `writeColorise` the raw gradient string (it is not a valid ANSI code — guard every writeColorise/comparison that could receive `backgroundColor`/`foregroundColor` when they hold a gradient). Stamping resumption is what restores the color.
   - `currentColor` history may hold gradient strings as the base entry; comparisons against them are string comparisons and fine, but nothing may print them raw.
5. Reset all new per-Write gradient state (cell colors, index, suppression flags) at the start of `Write` and in `String()`'s deferred cleanup, matching how `isTransparent`/`isInvisible` are handled.
6. `length` accounting unchanged. `Plain` mode: no escapes (existing `writeEscapedAnsiString`/`writeColorise` guards already handle this — verify stamping goes through them).
7. Hot path constraint: when neither channel is a gradient, the only added cost is the two `IsGradient` prefix checks — no allocations, no pre-pass.

### Tests (src/terminal/writer_test.go or a new writer_gradient_test.go)

Set up like existing writer tests (Init/shell formats, `Colors` assignment — follow existing test patterns). Cover at least:
- bg gradient, plain ASCII text: per-cell `48;2;...` codes present, first and last cell match `GradientCells` output endpoints; no single up-front bg escape for the segment.
- fg gradient same shape (`38;2;...`).
- both channels gradient.
- gradient + inline override mid-text (`<red,blue>x</>`): override wins during, gradient resumes at correct cell offset after.
- override of one channel only: other channel keeps stamping.
- wide rune (e.g. 漢): one escape, index advances 2.
- invalid gradient (one stop): falls back to solid first-stop rendering, no per-cell escapes.
- `length` identical for gradient vs non-gradient rendering of same text.
- Plain mode: output has no escapes.
Use `color.GradientCells` in tests to compute expected codes rather than hard-coding long escape strings, where it keeps tests readable.

### Benchmarks

Run `go test -bench . -benchtime 100x ./terminal/` before and after your change (git stash for the "before" run, or run on the unmodified function first) and report ns/op for the non-gradient benchmarks. Regression budget: noise only.

## Verification (must pass, run from D:\oh-my-posh\src)

```
go build ./...
go test -count=1 ./terminal/... ./color/... ./prompt/...
gofmt -l terminal/
golangci-lint run ./terminal/...   (if golangci-lint available)
```

## Reporting

Report: files changed, how the pre-pass is factored (shared logic story), test names + results, benchmark before/after numbers, and any decision the spec didn't cover (flag explicitly, do NOT silently expand scope). If the spec contradicts something you find, STOP and report. Do not touch src/prompt or src/color.

3. Gradient only renders in the DEFAULT path of `writeSegmentColors`. The transparent-foreground paths (both `fg.IsTransparent()` cases) must collapse any gradient via `GradientFirst()` before use — including the `bg.ToForeground()` call, which must never see a gradient string.
4. Inline overrides (`writeAnchorOverride` / `endColorOverride`):
   - An explicit override for a channel suppresses that channel's gradient stamping while active; cellIndex keeps advancing. Overriding only one channel leaves the other channel's gradient stamping active.
   - On restore (`endColorOverride`) back to a gradient base for a channel: do NOT `writeColorise` the raw gradient string (it is not a valid ANSI code — guard every writeColorise/comparison that could receive `backgroundColor`/`foregroundColor` when they hold a gradient). Stamping resumption is what restores the color.
   - `currentColor` history may hold gradient strings as the base entry; comparisons against them are string comparisons and fine, but nothing may print them raw.
5. Reset all new per-Write gradient state (cell colors, index, suppression flags) at the start of `Write` and in `String()`'s deferred cleanup, matching how `isTransparent`/`isInvisible` are handled.
6. `length` accounting unchanged. `Plain` mode: no escapes (existing `writeEscapedAnsiString`/`writeColorise` guards already handle this — verify stamping goes through them).
7. Hot path constraint: when neither channel is a gradient, the only added cost is the two `IsGradient` prefix checks — no allocations, no pre-pass.

### Tests (src/terminal/writer_test.go or a new writer_gradient_test.go)

Set up like existing writer tests (Init/shell formats, `Colors` assignment — follow existing test patterns). Cover at least:
- bg gradient, plain ASCII text: per-cell `48;2;...` codes present, first and last cell match `GradientCells` output endpoints; no single up-front bg escape for the segment.
- fg gradient same shape (`38;2;...`).
- both channels gradient.
- gradient + inline override mid-text (`<red,blue>x</>`): override wins during, gradient resumes at correct cell offset after.
- override of one channel only: other channel keeps stamping.
- wide rune (e.g. 漢): one escape, index advances 2.
- invalid gradient (one stop): falls back to solid first-stop rendering, no per-cell escapes.
- `length` identical for gradient vs non-gradient rendering of same text.
- Plain mode: output has no escapes.
Use `color.GradientCells` in tests to compute expected codes rather than hard-coding long escape strings, where it keeps tests readable.

### Benchmarks

Run `go test -bench . -benchtime 100x ./terminal/` before and after your change (git stash for the "before" run, or run on the unmodified function first) and report ns/op for the non-gradient benchmarks. Regression budget: noise only.

## Verification (must pass, run from D:\oh-my-posh\src)

```

go test -count=1 ./terminal/... ./color/... ./prompt/...
gofmt -l terminal/
golangci-lint run ./terminal/...   (if golangci-lint available)
```

## Reporting

Report: files changed, how the pre-pass is factored (shared logic story), test names + results, benchmark before/after numbers, and any decision the spec didn't cover (flag explicitly, do NOT silently expand scope). If the spec contradicts something you find, STOP and report. Do not touch src/prompt or src/color.

### How the pre-pass is factored (shared-logic story)
`countVisibleCells(txt, startHyperlink)` is the single source of truth for the cell count, sharing the same primitives the streaming loops use (`scanAnchor`, the `hyperLinkStart/Text/TextEnd/End` constants, `runewidth.RuneWidth`, the "link" no-text fallback rule). It mirrors what `write()` counts toward `length`, so `color.GradientCells` always gets the exact count the loop will consume.

For the streaming loop itself, I split `Write`'s inner loop into two functions: `writeBody` (byte-for-byte the original loop, calling `write(s)`) and `writeBodyGradient` (identical shape, but calls `writeVisibleRune(s)` and adds 3 lines to the hyperlink-fallback branch). `Write` picks one via a single `bgGradient || fgGradient` branch computed once, so the non-gradient path has zero per-rune branching — this was a deliberate call to duplicate ~35 lines rather than share one loop with an inline `if`, to hit the spec's "only added cost is two `IsGradient` checks" budget exactly rather than approximately. `stampGradient`/`activeBackground`/`activeForeground`/`clampCellIndex`/`collapseGradientFirst` are small shared helpers used by both the writer and the override/transparent guard sites.

### Decision not covered by spec (flagged)
Spec item 5 says to reset "cell colors, index, **suppression flags**" per Write/String. I did not add explicit suppression flags. Instead, suppression is *derived*: `stampGradient` compares `currentColor.Background()/Foreground()` (the override history's top, or the base color when empty) against the original `backgroundColor`/`foregroundColor`. Because `writeSegmentColors` always pushes the base (possibly-gradient) colors onto `currentColor`, and overrides push different literal colors, equality naturally tracks "is this channel currently overridden" — and `endColorOverride`'s `Pop()` naturally restores it. This is simpler and provably correct given how `color.History` already models push/pop, so I skipped the redundant boolean state.

One real bug this surfaced and fixed: `endColorOverride` and `writeAnchorOverride`'s transparent-invert branches could try to `writeColorise`/`.ToForeground()` a raw `currentColor.Background()`/`backgroundColor` value that holds a gradient string — guarded all of them with `.IsGradient()` checks, per spec item 4.

### Tests (`writer_gradient_test.go`, all passing)
- `TestWriteGradientRendering` (table): bg-only gradient, fg-only gradient, both channels, wide rune (漢, confirms one escape + index+=2), invalid single-stop gradient (confirms solid-first-stop fallback, no per-cell escapes)
- `TestWriteGradientOverrideBothChannels` — inline `<red,blue>` override mid-text, gradient resumes at correct cell offset after
- `TestWriteGradientOverrideOneChannelOnly` — fg-only override, bg keeps stamping uninterrupted
- `TestWriteGradientLengthMatchesNonGradient` — `Len()` parity between gradient and solid rendering of identical text
- `TestWriteGradientPlainMode` — `Plain=true` produces zero escapes
